title: Matter & Materials
objective: Students will explore matter & materials concepts including Dissolving & Solutions, Irreversible Changes, Reversible Changes and more.

steps.content: Welcome to an exciting lesson about Dissolving & Solutions!
Understand that some materials dissolve in liquid to form a solution, and describe how to recover a substance from a solution by evaporation
Time to try this out: Explain that dissolving means a solid mixes completely into a liquid to form a solution
Can you explain what happens when sugar dissolves in tea, and figure out how to get the salt back from salty water?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Dissolving & Solutions: Explain that dissolving means a solid mixes completely into a liquid to form a solution Describe how to recover a dissolved substance by evaporating the liquid
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Irreversible Changes!
Explain that some changes result in the formation of new materials and are not usually reversible, such as burning, rusting, and reactions with acid
Let's practice together! Define an irreversible change as one that creates new materials that cannot be changed back
Can you explain why you can't un-burn a piece of toast or turn rust back into shiny iron, because a completely new material has been made?

Great job! Today we learned about Irreversible Changes. Define an irreversible change as one that creates new materials that cannot be changed back Give at least three examples: burning, rusting, mixing bicarbonate of soda with vinegar
Let's explore Reversible Changes! Have you ever wondered about this?
Demonstrate that dissolving, mixing, and changes of state are reversible changes where no new materials are formed
Time to try this out: Define a reversible change as one where the original materials can be recovered
Can you explain that melting chocolate or dissolving sugar can be undone — you can get the original material back — because these are reversible changes?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Reversible Changes. Define a reversible change as one where the original materials can be recovered Give at least three examples of reversible changes: melting, freezing, dissolving, evaporating
Let's explore Separating Mixtures! Have you ever wondered about this?
Use knowledge of solids, liquids, and gases to decide how mixtures might be separated through filtering, sieving, and evaporating
Time to try this out: Choose the correct separation method for a given mixture (sieving for large particles, filtering for small, evaporating for dissolved)
If you has a mixture of sand, salt, and water, can they figure out a step-by-step plan to separate all three using a sieve, filter, and evaporation?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Separating Mixtures: Choose the correct separation method for a given mixture (sieving for large particles, filtering for small, evaporating for dissolved) Describe a multi-step separation plan for a complex mixture like sand, salt, and water
Let's discover the amazing world of Advanced Material Properties!
Compare and group everyday materials based on advanced properties: hardness, solubility, transparency, electrical and thermal conductivity, and response to magnets
Let me show you something cool. Define and test for at least four properties: hardness, solubility, conductivity, magnetism
Can you test materials and sort them by whether they dissolve in water, conduct electricity, or are attracted to a magnet?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Advanced Material Properties. Define and test for at least four properties: hardness, solubility, conductivity, magnetism Group a set of materials based on test results for each property
Let's discover the amazing world of Material Properties Vocabulary!

Use technical vocabulary to describe and compare material properties — conductor, insulator, thermal, electrical, transparent, opaque, translucent, soluble, insoluble, magnetic, flexible, rigid, density — and apply these terms precisely when selecting and justifying materials for particular purposes
Time to try this out: Classify a set of materials as electrical conductors or insulators and explain why using the correct terms
If you had to choose a material to make a window, could they use words like 'transparent' and 'insulator' to explain their choice — not just say 'you can see through it'?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Material Properties Vocabulary: Classify a set of materials as electrical conductors or insulators and explain why using the correct terms Use 'transparent', 'translucent', and 'opaque' correctly and distinctly in descriptions
